what_it_does: This file declares a UI-contract ledger entry for the `code-review` resource. It documents three native or hosted review surfaces—local `/code-review` and `/security-review` slash commands, Anthropic-managed GitHub Code Review, and CI `claude -p`—along with their output schema, a planned `code-review.act` operation, error vocabulary, and an explicit statement that the company currently exposes no review endpoint.
observations: A refocus note at the top says previously cited wrapper endpoints were removed because they duplicated real Claude Code session behavior.
The identity section states there is no `code-review://` scheme; a review is keyed by the diff or pull request it covers.
Every surface is marked `planned` for the company, with repeated honesty that no company review face exists in runtime, MCP, or ops.
The managed GitHub Code Review surface is described as Anthropic-hosted, research preview, Team/Enterprise only, and unavailable for ZDR orgs.
The CI surface is called the genuinely real and buildable path: a self-hosted runner (`pipeliner`) plus the session fabric running `claude -p`.
A JSON schema defines the review output shape including `surface`, `findings`, `summary`, and `tuning`.
The state model is stateless; managed run states live in GitHub/Anthropic, not in company state.
The `code-review.act` op lists tasks, aliases, bindings for TUI/HTTP/CLI, consequences, and verification states all set to `unverified`.
Error codes `code-review.not-exposed` (501) and `code-review.managed-unavailable` (403) include teach text pointing to native and CI recovery paths.
standouts: The file is an anti-specification: its main signal is documenting what is NOT built and where the real capabilities actually live.
It explicitly separates native TUI commands, Anthropic-hosted managed service, and the company's unpackaged-but-buildable CI path.
It names the real buildable seam as `session.create` + `session.inject` + `headless-control.watch` rather than inventing a new company endpoint.
It repeatedly gates the managed service with research-preview, Team/Enterprise, and ZDR constraints.
conventions: Follows the contract-entry frontmatter pattern with `type`, `resource`, `summary`, `schemes`, `status`, and `relates-to`.
Heavy internal linking with `[[...]]` references to other corpus entries.
Labels everything `planned`/`unverified` instead of implying shipped functionality.
Breaks from optimistic product specs by repeatedly asserting the absence of a company endpoint.
concerns: The single `code-review.act` op bundles three very different mechanisms—interactive TUI, Anthropic-hosted SaaS, and CI CLI—under one seam, which may be too broad.
The request schema includes `session`, `diff_ref`, `pr`, and `focus` but all implementations are unverified, so it is purely aspirational.
The `managed-github` binding is described as a GitHub comment trigger, not an HTTP API the company controls, which could confuse callers.
The refocus note references internal terms like `R1-prime profile` and `R3 config_writer rail` without defining them in this file.
notes: A deliberately honest contract entry that maps three native/hosted code-review surfaces and admits the company currently has no exposed review endpoint, naming the CI `claude -p` path as the real buildable option.
questions: What is the exact mechanism and API of the session fabric's spawn/inject/watch path referenced throughout?
What defines a ZDR org and how is the managed-service exclusion enforced?
How are `CLAUDE.md`/`REVIEW.md` tuning files consumed by each surface?
What were the removed wrapper endpoints (`③④⑤ MCP tools`, `/api/config|dev|auto` bridge arms) and why were they originally listed?
What is `pipeliner` and what would packaging a review workflow on it require?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the file is an interpretive ledger entry that declares the code-review resource contract and also documents the gap between native/hosted capabilities and any company-exposed endpoint.
apparent_themes: Radical honesty about unbuilt or unavailable capabilities.
Native/hosted-first design rather than duplicating Claude Code behavior.
Clear boundary drawing between company, Anthropic-hosted, and CI-runner responsibilities.
Planned-state transparency and recovery-oriented error messages.
intention_signals: The file seems designed to stop consumers from assuming a company-hosted code-review API exists, to give the UI an accurate model of the real native surfaces, and to identify the CI `claude -p` path as the nearest buildable integration while aligning the contract with actual runtime reality.
novelty: It is unusual as a specification that primarily documents what is not implemented and where the real, external, or unpackaged capabilities live, including a 'refocus' admission that earlier wrapper endpoints were removed.
contribution: This file contributes a precise, self-aware data model and operation contract for code-review that a fused system would keep as the place recording that no company review endpoint exists, what the three real surfaces are, what output shape they produce, the planned `code-review.act` seam with its bindings and error vocabulary, and the buildable CI path via `claude -p` on the fabric.
summary_for_embedding: This file is a UI-contract ledger entry declaring the `code-review` resource as a planned bridge over three native/hosted review surfaces—local `/code-review`/`/security-review` slash commands, Anthropic-managed GitHub Code Review, and CI `claude -p`—with schemas, a planned `code-review.act` operation, error codes, and an explicit statement that the company exposes no review endpoint today.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to give the UI and system an honest, native-aligned contract for code-review capabilities while preventing false assumptions that the company hosts or proxies any review endpoint.
